content.assessment: This proposal targets segregation channels (zoning, choice, legal exemptions) that operate independently of the admissions exam and track age, implying a distinct cost-incidence structure: it likely requires legal/administrative resources rather than large per-pupil funding shifts, but its value for money is uncertain given the historical fragility of similar interventions. The lens finds no direct cost or incentive evidence linking this mechanism to the concentration finding in F-live-education_finance-04, though it addresses a plausible structural contributor to that concentration.
opportunities: Could be combined with admissions reform (T3) or pilot de-tracking (T6) to compound cost-effectiveness across complementary channels
strengths: Targets segregation drivers distinct from the admissions mechanism, potentially achieving equity gains without disrupting the existing track-financing structure
Legal review of anti-discrimination exemptions is a low-marginal-cost lever relative to structural schooling reform
threats: Legal challenge risk from prior Curia exemption ruling could impose litigation costs and delay implementation
Demographic contraction may reduce the pool of students and schools over which zoning reforms can meaningfully redistribute enrollment
weaknesses: Prior administrative desegregation efforts were only temporarily effective, suggesting limited durable value for money from zoning/choice levers alone
Centralization under KLIK constrains local implementation capacity, raising the effective administrative cost of executing zoning changes
